title: Maritime museum

infobox.value: (sometimes **nautical museum**) is a museum specializing in the display of objects relating to ships and travel on large bodies of water. A subcategory of maritime museums are **naval museums**, which focus on [navies](/wiki/Navy "Navy") and the military use of the sea.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Type of museum related to ships

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article includes a list of [general references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#General_references "Wikipedia:Citing sources"), but **it lacks sufficient corresponding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")**. Please help to [improve](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Reliability "Wikipedia:WikiProject Reliability") this article by [introducing](/wiki/Wikipedia:When_to_cite "Wikipedia:When to cite") more precise citations. *(November 2013)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Wisconsin_museum.JPG/330px-Wisconsin_museum.JPG)](/wiki/File:Wisconsin_museum.JPG)

[USS *Wisconsin*](/wiki/USS_Wisconsin_(BB-64) "USS Wisconsin (BB-64)") is one of four *Iowa* class battleships opened to the public as a museum (berthed at [Nauticus](/wiki/Nauticus "Nauticus") in Norfolk, VA)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/PolandSzczecinSeaMuseum.JPG/330px-PolandSzczecinSeaMuseum.JPG)](/wiki/File:PolandSzczecinSeaMuseum.JPG)

[Maritime Museum](/wiki/National_Museum,_Szczecin "National Museum, Szczecin") in [Szczecin](/wiki/Szczecin "Szczecin"), [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/Museo_mar%C3%ADtimo_%C3%93sv%C3%B6r%2C_Bolungarv%C3%ADk%2C_Vestfir%C3%B0ir%2C_Islandia%2C_2014-08-15%2C_DD_066.JPG/330px-Museo_mar%C3%ADtimo_%C3%93sv%C3%B6r%2C_Bolungarv%C3%ADk%2C_Vestfir%C3%B0ir%2C_Islandia%2C_2014-08-15%2C_DD_066.JPG)](/wiki/File:Museo_mar%C3%ADtimo_%C3%93sv%C3%B6r,_Bolungarv%C3%ADk,_Vestfir%C3%B0ir,_Islandia,_2014-08-15,_DD_066.JPG)

A maritime museum located in the village of [Bolungarvík](/wiki/Bolungarv%C3%ADk "Bolungarvík"), Vestfirðir, Iceland showing a double 19th century fishing base, a salt hut, a fish drying area, a drying hut and a typical fishing boat of the time.

A **maritime museum** (sometimes **nautical museum**) is a museum specializing in the display of objects relating to ships and travel on large bodies of water. A subcategory of maritime museums are **naval museums**, which focus on [navies](/wiki/Navy "Navy") and the military use of the sea.

The great prize of a maritime museum is a historic ship (or a replica) made accessible as a [museum ship](/wiki/Museum_ship "Museum ship"), but as these are large and require a considerable budget to maintain, many museums preserve smaller or more fragile [ships or partial ships within the museum buildings](/wiki/Ships_preserved_in_museums "Ships preserved in museums"). Most museums exhibit interesting pieces of ships (such as a [figurehead](/wiki/Figurehead "Figurehead") or [cannon](/wiki/Cannon "Cannon")), [ship models](/wiki/Ship_model "Ship model"), and miscellaneous small items associated with ships and shipping, like [cutlery](/wiki/Cutlery "Cutlery"), [uniforms](/wiki/Uniform "Uniform"), and so forth.

[Ship modellers](/wiki/Ship_model "Ship model") often have a close association with maritime museums; not only does the museum have items that help the modeller achieve better accuracy, but the museum provides a display space for models larger than will comfortably fit in a modeller's home, and of the museum is happy to take a ship model as a donation. Museums will also commission models.

There are thousands of maritime museums in the world. Many belong to the [International Congress of Maritime Museums](/wiki/International_Congress_of_Maritime_Museums "International Congress of Maritime Museums"), which coordinates members' efforts to acquire, preserve, and display their material. There is a risk that too many maritime museums might dilute the experience for the public, while a poorly managed museum might put other municipalities off from the idea of hosting such a museum.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

At 80 acres (32 ha) the [Chatham Historic Dockyard](/wiki/Chatham_Historic_Dockyard "Chatham Historic Dockyard") in Kent, UK can lay claim to being the largest maritime museum in the world, incorporating numerous dockyard buildings, including a 1/4 mile long ropewalk, spinning rooms, covered slips, dry docks, smithery, sail loft, rigging house, mould loft, church, as well as three historic warships, it is the best preserved dockyard from the Age of Sail. However, the UK's [National Maritime Museum](/wiki/National_Maritime_Museum "National Maritime Museum") in Greenwich is also a contender, with many items of great historical significance, such as the actual uniform worn by [Horatio Nelson](/wiki/Horatio_Nelson "Horatio Nelson") at the [Battle of Trafalgar](/wiki/Battle_of_Trafalgar "Battle of Trafalgar"). The largest in the United States of America is 19 acres (7.7 ha), [Mystic Seaport](/wiki/Mystic_Seaport "Mystic Seaport") in Mystic, Connecticut; it preserves not only a number of [sailing ships](/wiki/Sailing_ship "Sailing ship"), but also many original seaport buildings, including a [ship chandlery](/wiki/Ship_chandlery "Ship chandlery"), [sail loft](/wiki/Sail_loft "Sail loft"), [ropewalk](/wiki/Ropewalk "Ropewalk"), and so forth.

A recent activity of maritime museums is to build replicas of ships, since there are few survivors that have not already been restored and put on display. Another is operating a [museum harbour](/w/index.php?title=Museum_harbour&action=edit&redlink=1 "Museum harbour (page does not exist)"), most notably in Germany and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") but elsewhere too, that offers mooring to privately owned historical vessels, which can be watched but not boarded.

## Preservation of ships

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maritime_museum&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Preservation of ships")]

The preservation of ships in museums ensures that ancient and historic vessels are preserved for posterity in optimum conditions and are available for academic study and for public education and interest.

Remains of ancient and historic ships and boats can be seen in museums around the world. Where a ship is in a good state of preservation it can sometimes act as a museum in its own right. Many [museum ships](/wiki/Museum_ships "Museum ships"), such as [HMS Victory](/wiki/HMS_Victory "HMS Victory") are popular tourist attractions. Some ships are too fragile to be exposed outdoors or are incomplete and must be preserved indoors. The remains of the [Mary Rose](/wiki/Mary_Rose "Mary Rose") for example are kept in a purpose designed building so that [conservation](/w/index.php?title=Conservation_and_restoration_of_watercraft&action=edit&redlink=1 "Conservation and restoration of watercraft (page does not exist)") treatment can be applied.

In some cases, archaeologists have discovered traces of ships and boats where there are no extant physical remains to be preserved, such as [Sutton Hoo](/wiki/Sutton_Hoo "Sutton Hoo"), where museum displays can show what the vessel would have looked like, although the vessel itself no longer exists.

## Notable maritime museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Maritime_museum&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Notable maritime museums")]

See also: [List of Transport Museums](/wiki/List_of_transport_museums "List of transport museums")
